standaloneCandidates.title: December 12, 1994 letter from Strobe Talbott to Yuri
standaloneCandidates.selectedReason: Talbott private letter to Yuri on U.S.-Russian relations after Budapest; Bosnia appears as one of the linked strategic issues.
standaloneCandidates.snippet: of Russia, the maintenance of favorable trends in U.S.-Russian relations is, quite properly, an important factor in our calculations on how to handle other issues (NATO expansion, Bosnia, -nonproliferation, the containment of rogue states, etc.). That doesn't mean giving Russia a veto on any matter; it simply means that none of tbese issues exists in isolation, that we must not discount or, worse, ignore the Russian factor, and that we have to base our foreign policy on a clear sense of priorities and a recognition of how they/ interrelate and interact. This assumption is not correct. Russian participation in IFOR could be of modest domestic political benefit for yeltsin, but only on the basis laid down by the Russians in recent talks with us: a de facto separate-but-equal role for their contingent plus an effective Russian veto of IFOR decisions on use of force. records.title: December 12, 1994 letter from Strobe Talbott to Yuri
records.description: State Department FOIA record C09000052, dated 12/12/1994, released in the May 2022 FOIA Library release, is titled "December 12, 1994 letter from Strobe Talbott to Yuri". The released text begins: "Like the last such letter I wrote you, quite a while ago, this is an entirely private communication. It goes from my head to your eyes without passing through any clearance procedures or any intermediaries, other than Jim's willingness to play postman."

records.snippet: of Russia, the maintenance of favorable trends in U.S.-Russian relations is, quite properly, an important factor in our calculations on how to handle other issues (NATO expansion, Bosnia, -nonproliferation, the containment of rogue states, etc.). Bob just phoned to report that the Contact Group had a quite productive meeting today."
records.snippet: . That does not mean we are contemplating giving up on the Contact Group: it has been a positive and proven mechanism for dealing with other aspects of the challenge in the former Yugoslavia, and we also value it as a mechanism in which the U.S. and Russia are making good on the concept of cooperation and partnership. Whatever the outcome in Rome, the U.S. will stay engaged with the Contact Group. If, as I indicated we are considering, we were to intensify our own independent diplomacy toward Belgrade and Pristina, we will make every effort to keep it coordinated with the efforts of the Contact Group and all its members. The released text begins: "Craigv SUBJECT: Continuing Our Dialogue on Russia Policy I agree with your opening point: Our principal differences are rooted more in terminology and typology than in prescription and policy. records.title: January 20, 1997 Note From Strobe Talbott To Barbara Larkin
records.description: State Department FOIA record C09000071, dated 1/20/1997, released in the Jan 2024 FOIA Library release, is titled "January 20, 1997 Note From Strobe Talbott To Barbara Larkin". The released text begins: "We're mighty lucky to have him as a colleague. He's with me in Moscow this week."
records.snippet: im as a colleague. He's with me in Moscow this week. I'll pass along your kudos. On another matter, Sen. Bill Roth has written me asking about setting up an Executive-Legislative "contact group" on NATO enlargement. (See attached.) WouId you please let his office know that I got this just as I was leaving for Moscow, but I'm eager to follow up. I'll be grateful to you for your advice on what next steps we should take in this regard. See you on my return. PS: I had a good talk with Wendy Monday evening; she's very supportive on the various points we talked about the other day. PSS: I have a full supply of Reese's, thanks to you, on board the Moscow Mission aircraft!

records.snippet: ite a few, but single out one in particular: Joseph Brodsky. When Joseph was expelIed from the Soviet Union in 1972, Brooke and I were reporters in Eastern Europe. We flew up from Belgrade to Vienna to interview him. He was staying with W. H. Auden and met us at a café near our hotel where together, we tested various exotic aperitifs, about which he made up puns in two languages, one of which in those days was only very approximately English. Several months later, Brooke and I spent New Year's Eve with Joseph in Venice, where we experimented with various brands of grappa. His English had improved, and the word plays were even more spectacu]ar. He later wrote a poem about our holiday together
